one_line_takeaway: SAFE reformulates machine unlearning under sequential (streaming) deletion requests as a dynamic-regret problem and uses an incrementally-updated Gaussian posterior-shift approximation plus single-step gradient updates from the original model to achieve accuracy comparable to full retraining while cutting per-round time by roughly 30% to 9x versus other streaming/batch unlearning baselines, backed by an O(sqrt(T)+V_T) regret bound without convexity assumptions.
key_finding: On Table 1 (Random Subset Unlearning, avg%±std% over 10 seeds), SAFE attains the best result in 10 of 16 evaluated metric/dataset cells and second-best in 4, and has the best (lowest) average rank R on all four datasets (e.g., MNIST: SAFE R=1.75 tied with Unroll R=1.75, both beating CaMU R=3.25, Desc R=3, Lcode R=5). SAFE achieves the best Forgetting Accuracy (FA) on every dataset and is closest to the Retrain reference on MIA attack accuracy for Fashion, CIFAR-10, and TinyImageNet. Numerically on MNIST: Retrain RA=99.68±0.05/FA=98.89±0.09/TA=99.00±0.05/MIA=79.25±1.14 versus SAFE RA=99.25±0.13/FA=98.89±0.12/TA=98.58±0.11/MIA=79.28±1.14, versus Unroll RA=99.24±0.22/FA=98.91±0.15/TA=98.61±0.19/MIA=79.27±1.15, versus CaMU RA=98.94±0.36/FA=98.72±0.79/TA=98.54±0.42/MIA=79.05±1.13, versus Lcode RA=96.26±1.95/FA=96.27±1.88/TA=95.60±1.94/MIA=78.79±2.22, versus Desc RA=98.78±0.53/FA=98.72±0.53/TA=98.27±0.46/MIA=79.24±1.14. On Fashion-MNIST, Retrain RA=96.44±0.15/FA=90.76±0.33/TA=90.40±0.15/MIA=79.57±0.51 (full row for SAFE and other methods was cut off in the extracted text beyond Lcode's Fashion row). On efficiency (Figure 1, average time over 20 rounds), SAFE cost 1.13s on MNIST versus the second-fastest, Lcode, at 1.66s; on CIFAR-10, SAFE cost 2.55s versus the second-fastest, Descent, at 12.90s (about 9x slower than SAFE); the paper states SAFE achieves the lowest time cost on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and TinyImageNet, at least 30% faster than the second-lowest-cost method.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'streaming unlearning', a setting where machine-unlearning deletion requests {F_t} arrive sequentially rather than as one batch, and formalizes it as a dynamic-regret / distribution-shift problem. It proposes SAFE (Stream-Aware Forgetting), which (1) maintains a retention-term gradient via a cheap recursive update (Eq. 15) instead of recomputing over the full original dataset D0, and (2) approximates the unreachable ideal unlearned model f(x;w*_t) needed for the forgetting term by reweighting the original model f(x;w0) with a density-ratio correction q_t^(y)(x) derived from class-conditional Gaussian statistics in a random-projected latent space, whose mean/covariance are updated incrementally (Eqs. 20-23) as data is removed. Each streaming round then performs a single gradient-descent step from w0 (retention gradient + forgetting gradient) plus a Gaussian perturbation for (epsilon, delta)-differential-privacy-style unlearning guarantees, avoiding repeated access to the original training set D0.
threat_model: Not applicable — this is not an attack/defense paper. There is no adversary model in the paper's own framing; the setting is a benign, cooperative one where legitimate users submit sequential data-deletion requests to a model owner (e.g., regulatory/privacy compliance under GDPR-style data-erasure). The only adversarial component used is a Membership Inference Attack (MIA), which is borrowed purely as an evaluation metric (following prior unlearning-eval protocols [5,45,15,27]) to check how close the unlearned model's MIA-attack accuracy comes to that of a fully retrained model — it is not the paper's contribution and not a new attack.
limitations: The paper itself notes: (1) the retention-term update still performs only a single gradient-descent step from w0 per round, which the authors acknowledge 'may introduce some performance trade-offs' though they argue theory shows this is still adequate with a properly chosen learning rate; (2) the forgetting-term approximation assumes feature-marginal shift between D0 and Dt is negligible or absorbed into a proportionality constant — an explicit simplifying assumption in deriving Eq. 18; (3) the Gaussian/latent-projection modeling of class-conditional densities is an approximation whose validity is checked only empirically (Mardia's normality test) rather than guaranteed in general; (4) the authors state a fully recursive strategy for the forgetting-term computation (beyond the Gaussian statistics) is left to future work to further improve efficiency; (5) Theorem 1's approximation-error bound grows with the cumulative deleted data size relative to |Dt|^1.5, implying degradation as more cumulative deletions accrue relative to the shrinking remaining dataset — an inherent scaling limitation for very long streaming horizons that the paper does not experimentally push to breaking point within the tested 20-round/400-point setting.

datasets_used: TinyImageNet (80K images sampled from Kaggle release)
quantitative_results: SAFE best or second-best in 14 of 16 (dataset x metric) evaluations in Table 1; best average rank on all 4 datasets
SAFE achieves best Forgetting Accuracy (FA) on every dataset tested
MNIST average rank R: SAFE=1.75, Unroll=1.75, Desc=3, CaMU=3.25, Lcode=5
Time cost (avg per round over 20 rounds): SAFE=1.13s vs Lcode=1.66s on MNIST; SAFE=2.55s vs Descent=12.90s on CIFAR-10 (~9x faster)
SAFE at least 30% faster than the second-fastest method on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and TinyImageNet (per Figure 1 caption)
Theorem 1 approximation bound: |R̃t(w) - Rt(w)| <= C * sum_i |Fi| / |Dt|^1.5, C = number of classes

Theorem 2 regret bound: cumulative unlearning regret <= O(sqrt(T) + V_T), compared against prior online-learning dynamic regret bounds of O(sqrt(V_T * T)) and O(T^{2/3}), and prior batch-unlearning streaming bounds of O(T^2) and O(T)
